title: Life After Death Row: Exonerees' Search for Community and Identity By Saundra D. Westervelt & Kimberly J. Cook. Rutgers University Press. 2012. 280 pages. $25.95 paper
abstract: Justice systems are imperfect. The guilty sometimes elude conviction and punishment, while innocents do not. The latter type of error is the more serious, and wrongful convictions in capital cases certainly represent the greatest of all miscarriages of justice. It is nearly impossible to imagine the plight of an innocent person—perhaps a family member, a neighbor, even one's self—unwittingly cast as protagonist in a true life Alfred Hitchcock drama, dispatched to death row to await execution after conviction for murder. Yet we know that this nightmarish experience has been a reality for scores of individuals during this country's modern death penalty era. Saundra Westervelt, of the University of North Carolina at Greensboro, and Kimberly Cook, of the University of North Carolina Wilmington, interviewed 17 men and one woman who shared this fate. Each was convicted of capital murder and sentenced to suffer punishment by death. Eventually, each was exonerated. On average, they spent nearly a decade in prison, mostly on death row, before regaining their freedom.
